It's like bathos in Guy Richie or the good Marvel movies, they know how to use it but more importantly they know when not to use it.

TL:DR: modern Hollywood are like techo barbarians operating a machine. They know the basic inputs, they know the basic levers to pull but they don't understand how anything actually works so it's mostly trial and a lot of error.

replies.author: @thermonuclearcollider4418
replies.text: That's because that score is mostly based on the original reviews from 1987, back when critics actually looked down on most genre flicks as they were not regarded as art. "Predator" was actually criticized for a having a very thin plot and being "very derivative" (their words). Critics from the 80s (and before that) could be snobbish assholes for sure, but at least they were NOT on the studios' payroll - actually, they seemed to be competing with each other at who could say nastier shit about flicks. And the more prestigious they were, the more anal they acted. If you look at the Rotten Tomatoes scores for movies from the past 10 years, you'd be fooled into thinking we're living in the best time there ever was for cinema, as ALMOST EVERY studio release is a masterpiece with a super-high score. Quite the contrary: almost every one of those flicks is pure drivel. Modern day critics are the embodiment of "Intellectual prostitution":

replies.author: @jaketheripper7385
replies.text: ​@Jamuka-k1h But a totally practical Yautja is also a dude in a big rubber costume which makes the creature appear slow, lumbering, and clumsy.  Kinda hard to believe that something that large and bulky is jumping through trees and moving silently through underbrush.  Combining practical with CGI like they do in the new film makes the Yautja quick, powerful, and threatening again.  They actually move like agile hunters and fight like skilled combatants who were plunged into a harsh culture of violence since birth.  The totally practical Yautja of the first couple films fought like brutish brawlers more than anything when they weren't attacking from the shadows, but most of the encounters occurred via ambush attacks which isn't necessarily what an "honorable" culture would do, bit I digress.  Besides, those Yautja were "blooded" hunters who had access to a cloak and more advanced technology.  Dek was an "unblooded" Yautja who hadn't earned the right to a cloaking device. Most of the violent scenes in the original films were shot using quick cuts and clever angles and framing to make the Yautja appear more dangerous, but the actual fight sequences tend to appear very stilted and choreographed.  In the first couple movies, the Yautja becomes much less dangerous and intimidating as soon as they lose the ability to cloak because they become big, slow foes with restricted movement that are really tough and strong, but seemingly easy enough to avoid if one just bobs and weaves a bit or simply retreats at a brisk jog.  I think it's cool to see the Yautja capable of some real, proper fighting for a change rather than heavily relying on their cloak/technology and ambush tactics or their overwhelming brute strength and the audience's ample suspension of disbelief.

replies.author: @motsognir
replies.text:
@will_sketch Talk about a biiiiig swing and a miss.
Disney's princess stories deal with bad stepmothers. One of the key elements of those stories is literally focusing on the nature of the relationship of a woman and a surrogate child that she didn't birth.
Cinderella exemplifies that most of all, by having the Evil Stepmother favor her biological daughters over her stepdaughter.

Many people can attest that there's a fundamental difference in having a relationship with one's mom and one's stepmom.
My mom is a great person. My stepmom was an immature patty girl who made everyone's lives miserable.

By conferring parental abuse to a stepparent, it changes the dynamic considerably. Characters rarely have to deal with a bad biological mother, but a bad biological dad is a constant in fiction.

replies.text: ​@Pythonogram85There were some good scenes and good parts in the later Predator movies, but the original can't be beat. The real question is why are they so hell bent on making so many sequels and add-on movies to the franchise? Seriously, they are like on the 10th (?) one already. Just proves they can't come up with new concepts and are only doing this to capitalize on the originals for cash. That's one reason why the original was so good. It was a totally new movie with a new creature that was never done before. Same with Alien and Terminator. Predator was also filmed for real in a jungle. Nothing was fake. The Predator actually looked menacing too, not some toned down thing made for children. Also they added stuff in here from other franchises...an android - Alien/Aliens and some Yoda like looking creature from Star Wars. That's just lame.

Now I grant you, AVP was OK for what it was, but don't even think of comparing it to Alien and Sigourney Weaver to Lathan in that one. You also have to acknowledge her in Aliens which is arguably the best sequel ever made or right up there. Those two movies blow AVP out of the water hands down. You're absolutely clueless if you think otherwise. Lathan was ok in AVP, but come on, there is no way she beats Weaver and AVP beats either of those two movies on any level. author: @HardAsStoneRN
text: To me the last truly good Predator movie was 2010's Predators. A stacked cast, pretty impressive visuals, some pretty decent action, and some really cool Predator designs including bringing back the OG design. It was also pretty good considering the fact this was coming after 3 disappointing predator movies and I think this concept of multiple people mysteriously appearing on the Predator's planet and trying to survive is a cool concept that should be tried again, albeit I think with a different studio running it and as a tv show to flesh it out more.

replies.author: @MarkDeSade100
replies.text: ​@FrawgfithAmblose Because if you're writing sci-fi, and you invent an alien species for your work, that species is meant to represent a certain idea or concept. The Vulcans of Star Trek are meant to show what humanity could be if we weren't constantly distracted by our emotions and could be totally rational all the time. The Klingons represent the barbarity of our history with their warlike nature, but also the sense of honor that we developed to mitigate that. An alien species needs to stick to its theme to be consistent. If you want characters who are all different and unpredictable, just write about humans.

replies.author: @motsognir
replies.text:
@DrTracer22 It isn't consistent, but there is a common thread of the females being used as early examples of girlbossing even years before Woke took off.
Some stories emphasize them as larger, in other cases they're not larger but they are smarter.
All-in-all, they're a novelty whenever adapted material wants to explore the "deadlier of the species" trope by making the females ironically more dangerous than the men, and playing upon the long exhausted lie that females are smarter than the males, which only exists among humans as a pathetic way to give women a balancing trait with men being physically stronger.

Most writers just aren't ready to admit that just because women are weaker than men, it doesn't mean they excel in any other area. Nature isn't balanced like RPG stats.
Sometimes nature is just unfair, and women have nothing over men even as men have traits over women.

author: @turtureels
text: Quite a bold step to have a predator protagonist. I think it worked great! Yautja are not orcs, which are the personification of pure evil. They are instead a culture of warriors and hunters that have families and traditions. They understand honor, are fallible and and have a capacity for discerning right from wrong (within their own cultural limits). What I took from this movie was: Family is the one that has your back, not necessarily the one you are born into. This was an enjoyable movie.

replies.author: @DannyKnightblade45
replies.text: ​@andrewcrus That's false. The plot is an alien hunter, after witnessing his brother's brutal murd3r, goes on a mission to slay a monsterous beast to fulfill a vow he made to his brother. Along the way, he reluctantly teams up with a synthetic being and an alien animal who help him become an even greater, more intelligent, more vicious hunter. It's certainly not like The Lion King.

replies.author: @andrewcrus
replies.text:
@DannyKnightblade45 A worthless teenager, whom his father wanted to kill while he was asleep, was locked up by his brother on a ship that was supposed to fly him to his death. The father kills the older brother for wanting to kill the younger brother in an alternative way. The younger brother can see all this because, apparently, their highly developed civilization (living in huts) has made an indestructible glass door on the ship specifically for this purpose.

On the plane of death, where every blade of grass is deadly dangerous, it is very scary during the day, but at night you can walk around as calmly as in your own kitchen.

On this planet, the younger brother finds the killer android with the personality of a capricious 5-year-old girl and a pet with big kind eyes.

The enemies in this film die not because the predator is incredibly strong, but because the laws of physics and logic bend around the main characters, and suddenly a half-meter sword inflicts three-meter cuts, and the legs of a broken androids without eyes can not only navigate the terrain, but also fight a platoon of similar unbroken killer androids.

At the killer androids' base on the deadly planet, there are no cameras, no patrols, no minefields around, no communication, no military equipment, but there is a forklift robot that can be used to destroy company property for fun.

And after 90% of the enemies killed by the android's legs, the main character returned home so very well-trained he was able to kill the most experienced warrior without any effort, simply because he is so special.

Does that sound more like a plot?

replies.author: @huslnflo13
replies.text: I think this may be only the SECOND time in history ive disagree with the DRINKER, but Badlands was pretty great! A much needed deviation from EVERYTHING else that we've seen at the theater this year except for BTTF in IMAX. It was fun and exciting. The humor wasn't overly done and to be completely honest the Predator formula was well worn out, so honestly why NOT try something new. Its not like they were changing Th Godfather or Rocky for fucks sake. It the PREDATOR and there hasn't been a good one in a LOOOONG time. I for one did not like Prey as the idea of a 100lb girl with primitive tools out classsing, out strategizing, and out muscleing, the fucking PREDATOR didnt quite sit well with me. I digress. Badlands is EXACTLY what we go to the theater for. Loud and intense action, funny moments, eye candy special effects and basically something new thay hasn't been done a billion times over. My opinion? Money well spent!! Oh and BTW Mando and Grogu is SOOOOO GONNA SUCK ASS!

author: @garron6775

A critique of Walt Disney was given by Tolkien in a 1964 letter to a fan:

I recognize [Walt Disney's] talent, but it has always seemed to me hopelessly corrupted. Though in most of the 'pictures' proceeding from his studios there are admirable or charming passages, the effect of all of them to me is disgusting. Some have given me nausea ."

Letter to Miss J.L. Curry, of 15 July 1964

author: @videogamehunter820
text:
The idea of revealing more about the Predators and their culture isn’t bad, as long as it is punishing, unforgiving, and badass.  Everything toxic masculinity loves.

Instead, they humanized a viscous killer who doesn’t believe in anything but honor and brutality.  The film did the same thing to the bounty hunter concept in Star Wars.  They took what should have been a brutal, ruthless aspect of the galaxy and dumbed it down to kid’s cartoon, sanitizing everything that gave it an edge.

They practically made a whole movie out of the ‘Predator’s Teenage Son’ parody from many years ago.

author: @dustydesertdisciple6290
text: Predator culture is rooted in honor, doesn't matter if dudes a runt, if he shows honor he will be honored, plus he wouldn't give af about any ally that couldn't fight and show the same honor in combat, the youtjas culture is so Kool, and this is an abomination.

replies.author: @gregorygreenwood-nimmo4954
replies.text: This is   good point - Predator culture is not about how much a Yautja can bench press, only about their skill and determination in the hunt. If anything, a smaller, less physically able Predator who was able to use their wits to offset their physical disadvantage in the hunt would be accorded more respect, since over coming a limitation to take trophies would be more challenging and so more honourable in their eyes. Dek would not be treated as a pariah merely for being smaller, but only if he failed to show honour in the hunt. Still, it is hardly surprising that Disney doesn't understand Predator culture and so made this mistake. I doubt most of the people behind this abomination of a movie even watched the iconic 1987 original or the unfairly underrated Predator 2, still less made an in depth study of the lore that has built up around the IP over the last four decades.

​@XFGHL78E People dunk on The Drinker because he's a dishonest and lazy reviewer who makes weak points, over simplifies movies and occasionally flat out lies about them, and uses the same annoying memes over and over again in his piss poor reviews. 
Weyland-Yutani has been around for a very long time. It's not hard to believe that in that time, people working within that corporation have had dealings with the Predator species and learned their language. Learning how to communicate with the Predator species is important, if not for diplomacy, then to understand what the Predators are doing or attempting to do to prevent them from doing it if their actions conflict in any way with the interests of the Weyland-Yutani corporation. Drinker complaining about Thia speaking the Predator language is just Drinker scraping the bottom of the barrel for the weakest criticisms of the movie he can possibly come up with.

author: @kadensanford4293
text:
"But I want scawy awien to die against edgy human guy for the 9th time :'(" 
dude is a loser.
Here were my gripes:
The movie had a handful of comedic moments (which I agree were lame, but it was literally like 6 times. Get over it. Millennials rule the film industry rn and it unfortunately shows.)
He was saved 4-5 times in the first act, which was lame, but made enough sense given the theme of evolving tactics to achieve survival.
He lost most of his kit upon landing on Genna. Story-wise it was cool, and him making up for it later was interesting and refreshing. I would've liked to see the cool new weapons put to greater use though.

I'm at 6:21 in his vid right now and frankly don't care to listen to more whining about "wokeness" and all the other bs that gets him clicks. If you want to see a Yautja fight a human and lose against all odds, go watch one of this film's FIVE predecessors with that EXACT SAME stale plotline. That doesn't even include either AvP installation. You've eaten the same shit slop for nearly thirty years and oh boy does it show.

Making a safe bet here to say that he comments about Dek's mom returning at the end, calling it some "woke girl-boss shit." I'd be pleasantly surprised if he didn't, but I'm not finishing the video, so I'll assume he does. Years of source material indicate that the Yautja are a matriarchal society so..... idk man, blow me. It was a dope setup for a sequel.

I could tear this old loser apart for hours, but I'm wasting my time leaving this comment anyway lol. I've been so tired of the hate for Badlands. It's the best Predator film yet- maybe second to the OG, but that story has been rinsed and repeated and milked so many times now that I'm over it. Badlands explores an actual dynamic character in Dek with complexity and depth and focuses on the coolest part of the Predator franchise- which is, believe it or not, the fucking Predator. Dek was dope. Action was awesome. CG looked amazing. Taming the baddest alien on the planet was a cool ass idea. Think of all the possibilities, and if you want a mediocre macho slasher film, watch the OG. Thank you, ladies and gentlemen, for joining me on this rant.
